coredata.dc:title:
A novel electrochemiluminescence sensor based on
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N - doped
carbon nanodots system for the detection of bisphenol A
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Libo
Yu, Bin
Zhang, Xueping
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description:
A novel
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
-based electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensing platform, using N-doped carbon nanodots (N–C dots) as co-reactant, was established for the sensitive and selective detection of bisphenol A (BPA). N–C dots not only could enhance the ECL signal, but also significantly improved the reproducibility and stability of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
ECL system. The developed ECL sensing system was sensitive to detect BPA based on its efficient quenching effect on
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots. The N–C dots-enhanced ECL mechanism and BPA-quenched ECL mechanism were investigated. Under the optimum conditions, the inhibited ECL intensity was in good linear relationship versus the concentration of BPA in the range of 0.03–1.0 μM with the detection limit of 10 nM. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for 25 successive measurements with one sensor was 1.1% and for eight different sensors was 2.5%, respectively. The fabricated sensing platform was successfully applied for the detection of BPA in baby bottle and nipple, and relatively satisfactory recoveries were obtained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bisphenol A
Electrochemiluminescence
N-doped carbon nanodots
Ru(bpy)3 2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title:
EC and ECL behavior of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots system and its feasibility for BPA detection
Detection of BPA in baby bottle and nipple

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM, (B) HRTEM, (C) Wide-scan XPS spectra, (D) FT-IR, (E) UV–Vis absorption and FL spectra, and (F) Dependence of emission on excitation of the prepared N–C dots. Inset of (E), photographs of N–C dots under white and 365 nm UV light, respectively.

(A) CV and corresponding ECL-potential curves of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
(a and a′),
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots (b and b′) and
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots/BPA (c and c′). Conditions: buffer, 50 mM pH 7.0 PBS;
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
, 0.5 mM; N–C dots, 0.1 mg mL−1; BPA, 50 mΜ; scan rate, 0.1 V s−1; PMT, 800 V. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra and (C) FL spectra of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
(a), N–C dots (b) and their mixture (c). (D) FL spectra of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
, N–C dots and ECL spectrum of their mixture. (E) UV–Vis absorption spectra of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots (a), BPA (b) and their mixture (c). (F) CV curve of 10 μM BPA in 0.1 M pH 7.0 PBS, scan rate, 0.05 V s−1.

Optimization of experimental parameters: (A)
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
concentration, (B) N–C dots concentration, (C) pH value and (D) concentration of PBS, (E) scan rate and (F) applied voltage.
Stability (A) and selectivity (B) of the propose method for BPA detection.
The possible enhancement mechanism by N–C dots and the quench mechanism by BPA of the system.
Performance comparison of the proposed sensor with other methods.

row.entry:
ECL
(
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots
)
dots)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: FLD, fluorescence detection; β-CD/ILCPE, β-cyclodextrin modified ionic liquid-based carbon paste electrode; CYP2C9-PAM/GCE, cytochrome P450 2C9 (CYP2C9) incorporated in polyacrylamide hydrogel films cast on glassy carbon electrodes; MMIPs NPs-CTAB/CPE, magnetic molecularly imprinted nanoparticles-cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide modified CPE; GR/Au-Tyr-CS/GCE, graphene/gold nanocomposite-tyrosinase-chitosan modified GCE; rGO–Fc–NH2/AuNPs/GCE, reduced graphene oxide (GO)-grafted (4-ferrocenylethyne) phenylamine (Fc–NH2)– AuNPs modified GCE; DBAE, 2-(dibutylamino)ethanol.
a, b intra-assay and inter-assay, respectively.

head.ce:title:
A novel electrochemiluminescence sensor based on
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N - doped
carbon nanodots system for the detection of bisphenol A

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Jilin University, Changchun, Jilin 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A novel
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
-based electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensing platform, using N-doped carbon nanodots (N–C dots) as co-reactant, was established for the sensitive and selective detection of bisphenol A (BPA). N–C dots not only could enhance the ECL signal, but also significantly improved the reproducibility and stability of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
ECL system. The developed ECL sensing system was sensitive to detect BPA based on its efficient quenching effect on
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots. The N–C dots-enhanced ECL mechanism and BPA-quenched ECL mechanism were investigated. Under the optimum conditions, the inhibited ECL intensity was in good linear relationship versus the concentration of BPA in the range of 0.03–1.0 μM with the detection limit of 10 nM. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for 25 successive measurements with one sensor was 1.1% and for eight different sensors was 2.5%, respectively. The fabricated sensing platform was successfully applied for the detection of BPA in baby bottle and nipple, and relatively satisfactory recoveries were obtained.

A novel N-doped carbon nanodots-enhanced anodic ECL of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
system was firstly developed for sensitive and selective detection of bisphenol A.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most important synthetic organic chemicals, bisphenol A (BPA) is predominantly applied in the production of polymers mainly including polycarbonate (PC) plastics and epoxy resins [1]. Existence of BPA is ubiquitous, from beverage cans, medical device to lining of cans such as food packaging, baby bottles, nipple and infant formula. However, BPA is an endocrine disruptor chemical (EDC) because of its estrogenic properties [2]. Many plastic containers made of PC materials may release toxic BPA into food products upon heating, leading to endocrine disorders. In addition, BPA is an environmental pollutant owing to its poor biodegradability. Therefore, it is very necessary to detect BPA at trace level.

Various analytical methods including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3], gas chromatography (GC) [4], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [5], fluorescence (FL) [6], chemiluminescence (CL) [7], colorimetry [8] and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [9] have been widely applied to quantify BPA. However, these developed methods are often related to complicated sample pretreatment, time-consuming process or poor detection limits. During the past two years, electrochemical techniques based on various modified electrodes [10–15] have been extensively fabricated to eliminate the potential disadvantages. However, the reproducibility and selectivity of electrochemical sensors are always restricted due to high overpotential of BPA oxidation at electrode surface [16]. Therefore, it is still an urgent need to develop novel method for determination of BPA with simple operation, high sensitivity, excellent reproducibility and good selectivity.

Eectrochemiluminescence (ECL), also well known as electrogenerated CL, combining advantages of electrochemistry and CL, have received great attention due to its high sensitivity, good selectivity and high reproducibility. As a typical ECL emitter,
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
, especially coupled with its co-reactant tripropylamine (TPA) has attracted growing interest in the sensing field. However, it is still required to develop alternative co-reactants to TPA due to its toxicity, volatile, low sensitivity at low concentration and dependence on electrode materials. Liu et al. [17] reported an efficient and environmentally friendly co-reactant of 2-(dibutylamino)ethanol (DBAE) for
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
ECL assays. Based on the quenching effect of BPA on
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
DBAE
ECL
system, Jiang et al. [16] developed an indirect method to detect BPA in river water. However, the reproducibility and stability still need to be improved.

Recently, application of carbon nanodots (C dots), especially heteroatom-doped C dots, has aroused broad concern due to their unique features including excellent water solubility, low toxicity, outstanding biocompatibility, and favorable optoelectrical properties [18]. Besides traditional fluorescence properties, C dots also exhibit remarkable ECL activity both at anode and cathode. Since the first investigation on ECL behavior of C dots [19] was reported, it has drawn more and more attentions. Chi and Chen's group perform lots of relevant work in this field [19–21], where C dots are served as emitter ions, and peroxydisulfate
(
S 2
O 8
2 −
)
or sulfite
(
SO
3
2 −
)
are commonly used as co-reactants. On the basis of its distinguished advantages, the novel C
dots
/
(
S 2
O 8
2 −
)
(
SO
3
2 −
)
system are interestingly investigated and applied to fabricate various ECL sensors [22–24]. However, application of C dots as co-reactant for
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
ECL is still in its infancy [25,26]. Currently, Long et al. [27] studied the related mechanism of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/ C
dots system in depth, and they found that benzylic alcohol units of C dots were contributed to their catalytic capability for anodic ECL enhancement of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
.

Herein, based on the efficient quenching effect of BPA on
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots ECL system, a new ECL sensor for sensitive and selective detection of BPA was fabricated. Due to its simplicity in preparation and modification process, the proposed method was very simple compared with traditional electrochemical sensors. More importantly, stability and reproducibility of the method were significantly enhanced upon the introduction of N–C dots. The relevant mechanism of N–C dots-enhanced and BPA-quenched ECL were also investigated.

Tris(2,2-bipyridyl) dichlororuthenium(II) hexahydrate (Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O) (Sigma–Aldrich), citric acid (Beijing Chemical Reagents Factory), urea (Genview), and BPA (Aladdin) were purchased and used without purification. Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, NaOH, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris), HCl, H3PO4, CH3COOH, H3BO3, quinine sulfate and all organic solvents and other reagents were of analytical grade and used directly without any treatment.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a TECNAI F20 field-emission TEM operated at 200 kV. A Thermo ESCALAB 250 instrument, coupled with Al Kα radiation (VG Scientific, UK) was used to perform the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements. Fourier transformed infrared spectra (FT-IR) were conducted on a VERTEX 70 FTIR. UV–visible adsorption spectra and fluorescence (FL) spectra were tested on a UV mini 1240 (Shimadzu, Japan) and a LS 55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin–Elmer), respectively. Electrochemical (EC) and ECL signals were recorded on a CHI 832 electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Apparatus Inc., China) equipped with a model MPI-E ECL analyzer (Xi′An Remax Electronic Science and Technology Co. Ltd., China). A traditional three electrodes configuration was composed of a 3 mm ITO working electrode, an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCL) reference electrode and a Pt wire counter electrode. The ITO electrode was homemade as follows: a piece of ITO conductive glass of about 4 cm length × 0.5 cm width was thoroughly sonicated in distilled water, ethanol and distilled water, respectively. After drying in the air, the conductive surface of the ITO was covered with a piece of about 3 cm length non-conductive adhesive, which had a 3 mm hole in the end to expose ITO conductive surface as working electrode.

N–C dots were prepared by microwave-assisted method reported by Qu et al. [28] with a little modification. Briefly, 4.5 g citric acid and 4.5 g urea were dissolved in 10 mL distill water and mixed thoroughly to form a transparent solution. Then the solution was heated for 5 min in a domestic microwave oven (800 W). After cooling down to ambient temperature, the products were dissolved in distill water and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant solution were filtered with 0.22 μm membrane and concentrated to dry. The obtained black solid products were defined as N–C dots and were accurately quantified to 10 mg mL−1 using distill water. For comparison, C dots were synthesized via the same procedure in the absence of urea.

The BPA detection experiments were conducted in aqueous solutions. For this,
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots mixture solution was used as the blank solution. Different amounts of aqueous BPA (10 mM) were added to the blank solution to give various concentrations of BPA. When cycling the potential from +0.5 ∼ +1.3 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) using cyclic voltammetric technique (CV), the EC and the ECL signals were collected simultaneously. The quenched ECL intensity was defined as ΔIECL, and ΔIECL = I0–I, where I0 and I represented the ECL intensity of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots in the absence and presence of BPA, respectively. All EC and ECL measurements were carried out at room temperature.
10 mM BPA stock solution was prepared by dissolving BPA in ethanol and other different concentration of BPA were obtained by diluting 10 mM BPA with doubly distilled water.

Practical samples of baby bottle and nipple were purchased from local supermarket. The treatment of samples was same to previous work [13]. In brief, 3.5 g and 4.0 g baby bottle and nipple were cut into small pieces and washed, which were then transfered into radius flasks with 35 mL and 40 mL absolute ethyl alcohol, respectively. The mixtures were stirred at 50 °C for 4 h and at room temperature for 24 h successively. After removing the pieces and filtrated with 0.22 μm membrane, the mixtures were concentrated to 1.75 mL and 2 mL, respectively. Finally, a certain volume of the obtained solution was added into the background solution for the BPA assay use.

In order to assess advantages of N-doped C dots in the EC and ECL investigation, C dots were prepared via the same procedure without urea. Results (Insets of Fig. S1A and B) show that C dots have poor solubility in water, while N–C dots exhibit excellent water-solubility upon the doping of N atoms, which should be attributed to the formation of N–H bonds on the surface of N–C dots [28]. On the other hand, although ECL intensity and system stability are greatly improved upon the addition of both C dots and N–C dots, N-doping provides better performance, especially system stability (Fig. S1).

TEM was conducted to characterize the morphology of N–C dots. Fig. 1 A illustrates that N–C dots are well dispersed and the size distribution is in the range of 1.9–4.0 nm, with the average diameter of 2.9 ± 0.4 nm (Inset of Fig. 1A). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1B) indicates the lattice spacing of the N–C dots is about 3.14 Å, which should correspond to the 002 facet of graphene layers, revealing the high crystallinity of the N–C dots.

Elemental composition of the as-synthesized N–C dots was analyzed by XPS measurements. Fig. 1C shows three peaks located at ca. 285.0, 400.0 and 531.0 eV, which are attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The result indicates that the prepared N–C dots are mainly composed of C, N and O atoms with the corresponding atomic percentages of 61.8%, 16.0% and 22.2%, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. S2A) of N–C dots reveals the existence of some characteristic groups including CC (284.3 eV), C–C (285.1 eV), C–N (285.7 eV), C–O (286.6 eV) and CN/CO (288.1 eV) (Qu et al., 2014); while the N1s spectrum (Fig. S2B) can be deconvoluted into three components located at 399.4, 399.9, and 401.4 eV, which are corresponding to the C–N–C, N-(C)3, and N–H bands, respectively [29,30].

FT-IR measurement (Fig. 1D) was sequentially carried out to confirm these groups. The broad peaks from 2753 to 3460 cm−1 indicate the presence of N–H, O–H and C–H bonds [31]. The peaks at 1717 and 1595 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO [32]. The peak appears at 1190 cm−1 is assigned to the characteristic adsorption of C–N or C–O bonds stretching vibration mode. Comprehensively, FT-IR observations agree well with XPS results, further demonstrating that various oxygen-containing groups are distributed on the surface of N–C dots.

From UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 1E), it can be observed that N–C dots exhibit two remarkable peaks centered at ca. 340 nm and 404 nm, which are assigned to the π–π conjugate structures of the matrix. The yellow aqueous solution of 0.1 mg mL−1 N–C dots emits blue-green fluorescence under UV light (365 nm) (Inset of Fig. 1E). Like most reported C dots [33–36], the emission wavelength of N–C dots is dependent on the excitation wavelength (Fig. 1F) with the maximum excitation and emission wavelength at 405 and 525 nm (Fig. 1E), respectively. Using quinine sulfate as standard, fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) of the N–C dots is calculated to be 13.6% (Fig. S3). These results agree well with previous reports [28,29], indicating that this method has a good reproducibility for the preparation of N–C dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
EC and ECL behavior of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots system and its feasibility for BPA detection
ce:section.ce:para:
Cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique was performed preliminarily (Fig. 2 A) to investigate the EC and ECL behavior of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots system and inhibition effect of BPA on the developed system. 0.5 mM
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
(Fig. 2A, a) give a pair of typical redox peaks at ca. 1.16 and 1.07 V, respectively. Introduction of 0.1 mg mL−1 N–C dots results in enhanced oxidation peak and weaken reduction peak of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
(Fig. 2A, b), indicating a remarkable catalytic effect of N–C dots on
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
oxidation [25]. However, upon the addition of 50 μM BPA, the oxidation current further increase, while the reduction current decrease slightly (Fig. 2A, c).

The corresponding ECL-potential curves indicate that 0.5 mM
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
emits relatively weak ECL signal (Fig. 2A, a′), and the ECL intensity is significantly enhanced upon the addition of N–C dots (Fig. 2A, b′). However, obvious decrease of ECL signal is observed in the presence of BPA (Fig. 2A, c′), demonstrating the potency of the expected method for BPA determination.

The N–C dots-enhanced and BPA-quenched ECL mechanism was investigated in detail. UV–Vis spectrum of 0.1 mM
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
(Fig. 2B, a) exhibits two adsorption peaks at 283 and 453 nm, which are corresponding to π–π* transition and metal-to-ligand charge transfer, respectively. 0.1 mg mL−1 N–C dots solution exhibits two characteristic bands at ca. 340 nm and 404 nm (Fig. 2B, b). While for
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots mixture solution (Fig. 2B, c), a rising and broader merging peak at ca. 421 nm is observed, indicating no direct chemical reaction occurs between
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
and N–C dots. Similarly, FL spectra (Fig. 2C) illustrate that both N–C dots and
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
preserve their FL features in the mixtures, further confirming the conclusion. Fig. 2D indicate that ECL spectrum of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots mixture agrees well with
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
FL spectrum, but separated from that of N–C dots, which clearly show that ECL signal of the mixture should result from
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
and is largely enhanced by N–C dots. Herein, although it is unclear which types of specific groups of N–C dots are responsible for their enhancement effect on
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
ECL, N–C dots play similar role to TPA and related mechanism can be described as Scheme 1 in the absence of BPA [27,37]:

Where N–C dots are used as co-reactant react with electrogenerated
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
3 +
to produce excited-state
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 + *
, which are capable to undergo ECL emission. However, the ECL can be inhibited upon the addition of BPA, and possible quenching mechanism was investigated via UV–Vis and CV techniques.

Fig. 2E and Fig. S4 shows the adsorption bands of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots/BPA mixture (Fig. 2E, c) are exactly the merging of their individuals (Fig. 2E, a and b), demonstrating that these species don't react with each other. CV result (Fig. 2F) reveals that BPA undergoes an irreversible electro-oxidation process with the peak potential at 0.563 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), which is far negative than that of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
3 +
. Therefore, it can be deduced that oxidation product of BPA may be responsible for their inhibition ability. Similar to previous reports [37,38], the inhibition mechanism may involve in an electron transfer process from exited-state
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 + *
to BPA oxidation product. Scheme 1 exhibits the possible reaction process, where, BPA can be electro-oxidized to generate 2, 2-bis (4-phenylquinone) propane (a) [39]. When BPA coexists with
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots system, electron of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 + *
move to intermediate a, resulting in reduced ECL signal.
In order to analyze BPA with high sensitivity and good selectivity, a series of experimental conditions were optimized systematically.

The quenched ECL intensity of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots by BPA (ΔIECL) in 0.1 M different medias including phosphate buffer (PBS), Na2CO3–NaHCO3, Tris–HCl and Britton–Robinson (BR) buffer were investigated firstly. Among these buffer solution, PBS provides relatively higher detection sensitivity and better stability. Thus, PBS buffer containing 20% (v/v) formaldehyde (Fig. S5) is selected for the subsequent studies.

Influence of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
concentration on ΔIECL was investigated over the range of 0.1–4.0 mM (Fig. 3 A). ΔIECL increase with higher
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
concentration up to 1 mM, but gradually decrease thereafter. Therefore, the optimum
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
concentration is chosen 1 mM.

The detection sensitivity of BPA strongly depends on N–C dots concentration. Fig. 3B illustrates that with the increase of N–C dots concentration, quenched ECL intensity by BPA increase. Moreover, stability of the novel ECL system also greatly improves with increasing N–C dots concentration, which should be contributed to high sensitivity for BPA detection simultaneously. However, inner filter effect of high level of N–C dots would cause side effects [25]. Consequently, taking sensitivity and stability into consideration, N–C dots concentration is therefore chosen as 0.4 mg mL−1.

Different from
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
TPA
system [40], both ECL signals of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots in the absence and presence of BPA continuously increase with pH value change from 5.0 to 12.0. However, the maximum ΔIECL is achieved at pH 8.0 (Fig. 3C), which may be attributed to the comprehensive effect of pH value on
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
consumption [40], N–C dots deprotonation [41] and BPA dissociation [16]. In addition, the system become growing unstable when pH value exceeds 8.0. Thus, the pH value is set as 8.0 for subsequent investigation.

PBS concentration also plays an important role on BPA detection. We can see from Fig. 3D that both too low and too high PBS concentration lead to smaller ΔIECL, and the largest ΔIECL can be obtained at 40 mM.

Fig. 3E exhibits effect of scan rate change from 20 to 500 mV s−1 on ΔIECL. It can be observed that the quenched ECL increase sharply in the range of 20–100 mV s−1, and reach to a plateau as the scan rate exceeds 150 mV s−1, which is chosen finally. Influence of applied voltage on ΔIECL (Fig. 3F) shows that the higher voltage is employed, the larger ΔIECL are obtained. However, an over-range phenomenon occurs when the voltage value exceeds 650 V. So, 650 V is selected eventually.

Under the optimal conditions: 40 mM pH 8.0 PBS containing 20% FA as buffer; 1 mM
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/0.4 mg mL−1 N–C dots as ECL indicator, scan rate is set as 0.15 V s−1 and 650 V of voltage is applied, the quenched ECL intensity of the novel ECL system by BPA, ΔIECL, increase linearly with BPA concentration from 0.03 to 1.0 μM (Fig. S6). The linear regression equation is ΔIECL = 2.577 [BPA] + 888.4 with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.991. The low of detection (LOD) for BPA analysis is determined to be 10 nM. It can be seen from Table 1 that sensitivity of the new ECL-based sense system is comparable to recently developed various EC sensors. However, in comparison with traditional chromatography (HPLC) or spectroscopy (FL, CL and SERS) detection methods, LOD for BPA detection is not very low. Despite all this, it is believed that the sensitivity of the ECL system can be further improved by combining with traditional chromatography methods such as HPLC or CE. More importantly, the proposed method is relatively stable, time-saving and simple, which avoids complex preparation and modification process.

Stability of the method was investigated by recording the ECL signals of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots solution in the absence and presence of BPA for 25 successive measurements (Fig. 4 A), and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) are 1.4% and 1.1%, respectively, indicating excellent stability of the method, which should be contributed to the introduction of N–C dots. On the other hand, RSD for 8 parallel measurements is 2.5%, adequately showing ideal repeatability of the system.

In order to evaluate selectivity of the established BPA sensor, effect of foreign substances including 50-fold 4-chlorophenol, p-nitrophenol, 2, 6-dimethylphenol, catechol, uric acid, 500-fold maltose, fructose, glucose and galactose on detection of BPA were studied. Results (Fig. 4B) demonstrate that this method owns good selectivity and that interference from these analytes for PBA detection can be ignored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of BPA in baby bottle and nipple
ce:section.ce:para: Practical application of the method was assessed by detecting BPA in baby bottle and nipple via standard addition method. Certain known amounts of BPA were added into the treated sample and the recoveries were estimated to be 90%–120% and 103.33–118.75% for baby bottle and nipple samples, respectively (Table S1). RSDs for 3 times parallel experiments ranged from 0.89% to 5.53%. These suggest that the developed method possesses potential applicability for BPA analysis in real samples.

In short, as a novel co-reactant of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
, N–C dots significantly enhance the ECL signal and stability of the system. The developed ECL sense platform based on
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots system for BPA detection is advantageous in simple preparation and modification process, high sensitivity, good stability and repeatability, and potential applicability.

In summary, a novel ECL sense platform for BPA indirect detection is firstly proposed based on their capacity of inhibiting effect on N–C dots-enhanced anodic ECL of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
. Upon the addition of N–C dots, sensitivity and stability of
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
ECL signal significantly promoted, which may be attributed to the reaction between N–C dots and electrogenerated
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
3 +
However, with further introduction of BPA into
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 +
/
N − C
dots system, the ECL emission is efficiently intercepted by BPA through electron transfer process from exited-state
Ru
(
bpy
)
3
2 + *
to electro-oxidation product of BPA. Under the optimum conditions, sensitive analysis of BPA is achieved in the range of 0.03–1.0 μM with detection limit low to 10 nM. Finally, the proposed method is successfully applied to detect BPA in real sample of baby bottle and nipple, and good results are obtained, indicating its potential prospect in the future.
The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21222505, 21475124) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2014T70301).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2015.08.055.
